candidate_problems.text: Is coalition-formation truly computationally irreducible in social coordination, or can institutions, protocols, or richer information environments compress the trust-and-affiliation problem enough to make boundary formation substantially less central? The key unresolved issue is whether irreducibility is a deep constraint or only a contingent feature of current social architectures.

What exactly distinguishes unavoidable coordination groupings from harmful tribalism? The conjecture risks collapsing all affiliation boundaries into one category, but the crucial open problem is to identify the structural properties—permeability, cross-cutting membership, error-correction, reversibility, violence potential, status closure—that separate benign clustering from destructive in-group/out-group dynamics.

Under what conditions do anti-tribal norms generate second-order tribalism versus genuinely reducing conflict across boundaries? The unresolved tension is that the conjecture predicts displacement of grouping, but not when displacement amplifies polarization, when it softens it, or what feedback loops make anti-tribal ideology become a new status marker.
